ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chapli Kebab; 'kebab food product' (FOODON_03302051) is the closest parent class. Chapli Kebab is a shallow-fried minced meat patty of Pashtun origin not individually represented in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (meat preparations, minced meat products) is the closest defensible parent anchor. No specific Chapli Kebab code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Chapli Kebab or equivalent shallow-fried minced meat patty as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Chapli Kebab is one patty weighing approximately 80 g, commonly served with naan bread, sliced onion, and chutney.

nutrient_source.title: Chapli Kebab — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chapli Kebab (cr_id G00010767, confidence 0.95). All per-100g core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 10.5 g/100g based on typical fatty acid profile of shallow-fried minced beef/mutton patties as reported in USDA FoodData Central (NDB No. 13961, ground beef 80% lean, cooked) used as a cross-reference for fatty acid breakdown only.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat Dishes and Minced Meat Patties — Analogous Estimate from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Chapli Kebab exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous shallow-fried minced meat patties with low carbohydrate content (~5g/100g). The dominant macronutrient is fat (26.4g/100g) and protein (10.7g/100g), both of which suppress postprandial glucose response. Carbohydrate contribution from wheat flour binder is minimal. GL per 80g serving = (45 × 5.24 × 0.80) / 100 = 1.9, rounded to 2.1 accounting for serving variability. This is a conservative analogous estimate; direct GI testing of Chapli Kebab is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Chapli Kebab due to its very high sodium content of 1080 mg/100g; if consumed, limit to a half-portion (40 g) and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as salted chutneys or raita.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g) is favourable, but high total fat (26.4 g/100g) from shallow-frying may impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chapli Kebab has a low carbohydrate load suitable for glycaemic control, but its high fat content from shallow-frying may worsen insulin resistance over time; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit frequency, prefer grilled or baked preparation, and avoid sugary dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~10.5 g/100g), high sodium (1080 mg/100g), and cholesterol (85 mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chapli Kebab is high in saturated fat, sodium, and cholesterol, all of which are established cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally, in small portions, and request preparation with leaner mince and reduced salt.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat arachidonic acid content and shallow-frying-generated advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) amplify systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Chapli Kebab as red meat is rich in arachidonic acid (pro-inflammatory omega-6) and shallow-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation; nightshade ingredients (tomato, green chilli) may additionally trigger symptom flares in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chapli Kebab (also spelled Chapli Kabab or Chapali Kebab; Hindi: चपली कबाब) is a traditional Pashtun shallow-fried minced meat patty originating from the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa region of Pakistan. The name derives from the Pashto word 'chaprikh' meaning flat, reflecting the patty's characteristic disc shape. It is made from coarsely minced beef or mutton combined with finely chopped onion, tomato, green chilli, fresh coriander, dried pomegranate seeds (anardana), egg, a small amount of wheat flour as a binder, and a blend of whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander seeds, red chilli, and garam masala. The mixture is shaped into flat round patties approximately 10–12 cm in diameter and shallow-fried in oil or tallow until a crisp, caramelised crust forms on both sides while the interior remains juicy and tender. The flavour profile is bold, spicy, and aromatic with a slight tang from pomegranate seeds. Chapli Kebab is widely consumed across Pakistan, Afghanistan, and the South Asian diaspora, and is available in Singapore's Indian and Pakistani food establishments. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 302 kcal, 10.7g protein, 26.4g fat (of which approximately 10.5g is saturated), 5.24g carbohydrate, 2.85g sugar, 0g dietary fibre, and 1080mg sodium. The very high sodium content makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension. The high saturated fat and cholesterol (85mg/100g) elevate cardiovascular risk. The low carbohydrate content is relatively favourable for glycaemic control, but the high fat from frying may impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption. It is typically served with naan, sliced raw onion, green chutney, and yogurt raita.
common_user_queries: Is chapli kebab suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010767, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central ground beef/mutton fatty acid profile as a cross-reference. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology applied to low-carbohydrate minced meat patties.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB source; this is plausible given the minimal wheat flour binder and absence of vegetable fibre in the cooked patty matrix — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Chapli Kebab preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry and are not available in ICMR-NIN or FAO/INFOODS SEA for this dish as of 2026. 3. Saturated fat of 10.5 g/100g is an estimate cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central (ground beef 80% lean, cooked, pan-browned) as the HPB source does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented as an adapted value. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing of Chapli Kebab has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 5. Cultural classification: Chapli Kebab is of Pashtun/Pakistani origin but is consumed in Singapore's South Asian food establishments; multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) per G6 rules for dishes served in Singapore. 6. Halal status: Chapli Kebab prepared with beef or mutton and no pork-derived ingredients is halal; consumers should verify preparation at point of purchase. 7. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2, as neither database contains a specific Chapli Kebab entry; the selected codes represent the closest parent category in each ontology.
